mainTopic: החזרת שטר בשני מודים · מציאת שובר
overview: (1) The Gemara resolves a contradiction regarding returning a found document when both parties admit to the debt, distinguishing between a healthy person (בריא) and a deathbed donor (שכיב מרע), and between a document written by the owner himself versus one written by his father. (2) The Gemara discusses the law of finding a receipt (שובר) for a debt, analyzing whether it should be returned to the husband if the wife admits to the debt, and deriving the law that a creditor can forgive a debt even if the document is in the hands of a third party. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara resolves a contradiction regarding returning a found document when both parties admit to the debt, distinguishing between a healthy person (בריא) and a deathbed donor (שכיב מרע), and between a document written by the owner himself versus one written by his father.

The Gemara discusses the law of finding a receipt (שובר) for a debt, analyzing whether it should be returned to the husband if the wife admits to the debt, and deriving the law that a creditor can forgive a debt even if the document is in the hands of a third party. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: Distinction between בריא and שכיב מרע
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces the fundamental distinction that resolves the contradiction between the Mishnah and the Baraita: one refers to a healthy person (בריא) and the other to a deathbed donor (שכיב מרע).
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא opens by resolving the apparent contradiction between the משנה and the ברייתא. It establishes that the two sources apply to different legal contexts: a gift from a healthy person versus a gift from a deathbed donor.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This distinction is the overarching framework for the entire sugya. A שכיב מרע has the unique ability to retract gifts, whereas a בריא generally does not. This difference drives the subsequent legal analysis.

keyTerms.meaning: In the case of a healthy person
In the case of a deathbed donor

steps.whatToRemember: The core resolution is the distinction between בריא and שכיב מרע.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that the גמרא will first explain the משנה as שכיב מרע and the ברייתא as בריא, but this will later be challenged.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction drives the entire legal analysis of document return and the ability to retract gifts.

phrases.english: This in a healthy person, and this in a deathbed patient.

rashi.hebrew: הא בבריא - שהיה שטר מתנת בריא דלא כתיב ביה כדקציר ורמי בערסיה כדין שכותבין בשטרי מתנות שכיב מרע:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Mishnah refers to a gift document from a healthy person (שטר מתנת בריא) which lacks the specific clause 'כדקציר ורמי בערסיה' that is customarily written in deathbed gift documents.

steps.title: Mishnah refers to שכיב מרע
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara identifies the Mishnah as referring to a שכיב מרע, who is capable of retracting a gift.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא begins its detailed explanation by assigning the משנה to the case of a שכיב מרע. It explains that the ruling to return the document applies here because the donor is capable of retracting.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The משנה states that if the giver says 'give it,' the finder should give it. The גמרא explains this applies to a שכיב מרע because he is 'דבר מיהדר הוא'. This sets up the need to explain why, despite his ability to retract, the document should still be returned.
steps.whatToRemember: The משנה's ruling to return the document is attributed to a שכיב מרע.
steps.whyThisMatters: This assignment is crucial for the subsequent explanation of why returning the document causes no loss.

phrases.aramaic: מַתְנִיתִין דְּקָתָנֵי: הָא אָמַר תְּנוּ נוֹתְנִין –
phrases.english: Our Mishna is precise: Here it says: Give, they give –
In a deathbed donor, it is a matter of retraction.

steps.triggerLanguage: מַתְנִיתִין דְּקָתָנֵי: הָא אָמַר תְּנוּ נוֹתְנִין – בִּשְׁכִיב מְרַע, דְּבַר מִהְדַּר הוּא.

rashi.hebrew: דבר מהדר הוא - שיכול לחזור במתנתו אם כתב שני שטרות השני זוכה שהרי חזר בו מראשון:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the term דבר מיהדר refers to the שכיב מרע's ability to retract his gift by issuing a second document, which would invalidate the first.

steps.title: Scenario of retraction via found document
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara outlines the scenario where a שכיב מרע might try to retract a gift by producing an earlier, undelivered document.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains the underlying concern: why might a שכיב מרע want the document returned? It posits a scenario where the donor wrote a document for Person A, changed his mind, then wrote and gave a document to Person B.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The donor wants the first document returned to Person A so that Person A can claim the property, effectively retracting the gift to Person B. The גמרא is setting up the explanation of why this scheme fails.
steps.whatToRemember: The donor's motive is to use the found document to retract a later gift.
steps.whyThisMatters: This scenario explains the potential for fraud and sets up the legal principle that the later document prevails.

phrases.english: We say: What is there to say?

phrases.aramaic: דִּלְמָא כַּתְבַהּ מֵעִיקָּרָא לְהַאי וְאִמְּלִיךְ וְלָא יַהֲבַהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ,
phrases.english: Perhaps he originally wrote it for this person and did not give it to him,

phrases.aramaic: וַהֲדַר כַּתְבַהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְיַהֲבַיהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ –
phrases.english: and then he wrote it for another person and gave it to him –

phrases.aramaic: הַשְׁתָּא קָא הָדַר בֵּיהּ מֵהָהוּא דְּיַהֲבַהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ.
phrases.english: now he is retracting from that one to whom he gave it.

steps.triggerLanguage: דְּאָמְרִינַן, מַאי אִיכָּא לְמֵימַר: דִּלְמָא כַּתְבַהּ מֵעִיקָּרָא לְהַאי וְאִמְּלִיךְ וְלָא יַהֲבַהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ, וַהְדַר כַּתְבַהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְיַהֲבַיהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ – הַשְׁתָּא קָא הָדַר בֵּיהּ מֵהָהוּא דְּיַהֲבַהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ.

steps.title: No loss if second gift was from a בריא
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains that if the second gift was given as a בריא, returning the first document causes no loss because the later document prevails.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא analyzes the legal outcome if the second gift was given by a healthy person. It concludes that returning the first document to Person A is harmless.
steps.deeperAnalysis: When both documents are produced, the later one (Person B's) wins. The appearance of the first document signals a retraction of the first gift, thus validating the second. Person B loses nothing.
steps.whatToRemember: In a dispute between two documents, the later one prevails, signaling retraction of the earlier one.
steps.whyThisMatters: This principle ensures that returning the document doesn't harm the second recipient.

phrases.english: If as a gift of a healthy person he gave it to him –
he has no loss,
for when two [deeds] emerge, the latter one acquires,
for this he retracted from the first one.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִי בְּמַתְּנַת בָּרִיא יַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ – לֵית לֵיהּ פְּסֵידָא, דְּכִי נָפְקָא תַּרְתֵּי, בָּתְרָיְיתָא זָכֵי, דְּהָא הֲדַר בֵּיהּ מִקַּמָּיְיתָא.

rashi.hebrew: אי במתנת בריא יהבה ניהליה - לההוא בתרא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Gemara's phrase 'אי במתנת בריא יהבה ניהליה' refers specifically to the second recipient (לההוא בתרא).

rashi.hebrew: לית ליה פסידא - בהאי שטרא דמהדרא ליה להאי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that 'לית ליה פסידא' means the second recipient suffers no loss when the first document is returned to the first recipient.

steps.title: No loss if second gift was from a שכיב מרע
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains that even if the second gift was given as a שכיב מרע, returning the first document causes no loss because the later document still prevails.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא extends the logic to the case where the second gift was also given by a שכיב מרע. It concludes that returning the first document is still harmless.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The same principle applies: the later document prevails, indicating a retraction of the first. Person B retains the property. Thus, in both cases, returning the first document is safe.

keyTerms.meaning: There is no loss in it

steps.whatToRemember: The principle that the later document prevails applies regardless of whether the second gift was from a בריא or שכיב מרע.
steps.whyThisMatters: This completes the explanation for why the משנה permits returning the document in the case of a שכיב מרע.

phrases.aramaic: אִי בְּמַתְּנַת שְׁכִיב מְרַע נָמֵי יַהֲבַהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ,
phrases.english: If even as a gift of a deathbed person he gave it to him,
there is no loss in it — for the latter one acquires,
for he has returned from the first one.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִי בְּמַתְּנַת שְׁכִיב מְרַע נָמֵי יַהֲבַהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ, לֵית בַּהּ פְּסֵידָא – דְּבָתְרָיְיתָא זָכֵי, דְּקָא הָדַר בֵּיהּ מִקַּמָּיְיתָא.

steps.title: Baraita refers to בריא
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara identifies the Baraita as referring to a בריא, who is not capable of retracting a gift.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא shifts to explaining the ברייתא, assigning it to the case of a בריא. It explains that the ruling not to return the document applies here because the donor is not capable of retracting.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ברייתא states that even if both parties admit, the document should not be returned. The גמרא explains this applies to a בריא, who is 'לא בר מיהדר הוא'. This sets up the need to explain why, despite his inability to retract, the document should NOT be returned.

keyTerms.meaning: He is not capable of retracting

steps.whatToRemember: The ברייתא's ruling not to return the document is attributed to a בריא.
steps.whyThisMatters: This assignment is crucial for the subsequent explanation of why returning the document causes a loss.

phrases.english: Because it is small in the baraita:

phrases.aramaic: אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁשְּׁנֵיהֶם מוֹדִים לֹא
phrases.english: Even though both of them admit, not
return, not to this one and not to that one –
healthy, that he is not one who retracts,

steps.triggerLanguage: כִּי קָתָנֵי בְּבָרַיְיתָא: אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁשְּׁנֵיהֶם מוֹדִים לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה – בְּבָרִיא, דְּלָאו בַּר מִהְדַּר הוּא,

rashi.hebrew: בבריא - כגון שהיה השטר זה הנמצא מתנת בריא שהוא זכה לשטר המאוחר לו שאינו יכול לחזור במתנתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the term בריא refers to a case of מתנת בריא, where the donor transferred a later document to the recipient and cannot retract the gift.

tosafot.hebrew: ברייתא בבריא דלמא כתבה להאי ולא יהביה ליה - היינו דלא כאביי דלדידיה אמאי לא נהדר כיון שהוא רוצה דעדיו בחתומיו זכין לו היכא דמטא שטרא לידיה ואביי יתרץ כדרב זביד דבסמוך:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the Baraita's ruling applies to a בריא who might have written the document for the recipient but not yet delivered it, and notes that this differs from אביי's view, who would require a different resolution similar to רב זביד's approach.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות highlights a conceptual disagreement with אביי regarding the status of the document in a מתנת בריא case, whereas רש״י simply defines the term בריא as referring to מתנת בריא without addressing the broader halachic implications.

steps.title: Scenario of fraud by a בריא
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara outlines the scenario where a בריא might try to fraudulently retract a gift by producing an earlier, undelivered document.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains the underlying concern: why might a בריא want the document returned? It posits a scenario where the donor wrote a document for Person A, changed his mind, then wrote and gave a document to Person B.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Since a בריא cannot legally retract, he plans to use the found document to trick the court. He will claim he gave the first document, and since it's earlier, Person A will acquire the property. The donor will then split the property with Person A, effectively retracting the gift to Person B fraudulently.

keyTerms.meaning: They will return the document to him

steps.whatToRemember: The donor's motive is to use the found document to fraudulently retract a later gift.
steps.whyThisMatters: This scenario explains the potential for fraud and sets up the legal principle that the earlier document prevails in this context.

phrases.english: We say:

phrases.aramaic: דִּלְמָא כַּתְבַהּ לְהַאי מֵעִיקָּרָא וְאִמְּלִיךְ וְלָא יַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ,
phrases.english: Perhaps he wrote it for this one originally and reconsidered and did not give it to him,

phrases.aramaic: וַהֲדַר כַּתְבַהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְיַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ –
phrases.english: and then he wrote it for another person and gave it to him –

phrases.aramaic: הַשְׁתָּא קָא הָדַר בֵּיהּ מֵהָהוּא דְּיַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ וְסָבַר:
phrases.english: now he is retracting from that one to whom he gave it and thinks:
I cannot retract, I will retract in myself,
phrases.notes: Meaning: I will use a trick to achieve the retraction.

phrases.english: I will tell them that I gave it to this one,
and they will return the document to him,

phrases.aramaic: כִּי הֵיכִי דְּכִי מַפֵּיק הַאי כְּתָבָא,
phrases.english: so that when this one produces the document,
that he who preceded acquires it.

steps.triggerLanguage: דְּאָמְרִינַן: דִּלְמָא כַּתְבַהּ לְהַאי מֵעִיקָּרָא וְאִמְּלִיךְ וְלָא יַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ, וַהְדַר כַּתְבַהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְיַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ – הַשְׁתָּא קָא הָדַר בֵּיהּ מֵהָהוּא דְּיַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ וְסָבַר: מִהְדָּר לָא מָצֵינָא הָדַרְנָא בִּי, אֵימַר לְהוּ דַּאֲנָא לְהַאי יַהֲבִתַהּ, וְנַיהְדְּרוּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ כְּתָבָא, כִּי הֵיכִי דְּכִי מַפֵּיק הַאי כְּתָבָא, דְּקָדֵים זָכֵה בֵּיהּ הוּא.

steps.title: Court's response to prevent fraud
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains the court's response: they will not return the document, but will instruct the donor to write a new one if he is truthful.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains why the ברייתא prohibits returning the document. The court will not return the found document to Person A to prevent fraud.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If the donor is truthful and wants to give to Person A, he should write a new document now. If he had already given to Person B, Person B incurs no loss because the earlier document (the new one for Person A) will prevail. Thus, the court prevents fraud by not returning the old document.

keyTerms.meaning: We will not give it to him

steps.whatToRemember: The court prevents fraud by refusing to return the old document and requiring a new one.
steps.whyThisMatters: This explains the ברייתא's ruling not to return the document in the case of a בריא.

phrases.english: Rather, we say to him:

phrases.aramaic: הַאי כְּתָבָא לָא יָהֲבִינַן לֵיהּ לְהַאי.
phrases.english: We do not give this document to this one.
Perhaps you wrote it,
but you did not give it to him,

phrases.aramaic: וִיהַבְתַּהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְקָא הָדְרַתְּ בֵּיהּ.
phrases.english: and you gave it to another person and you are now retracting it.

phrases.aramaic: אִי לָא יְהַבְתַּהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְקָא
phrases.english: If you did not give it to another person and you
want that you give it to this one,

phrases.aramaic: כְּתֹב לֵיהּ הַשְׁתָּא כְּתָבָא אַחֲרִינָא וְיַהֲבֵיהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ,
phrases.english: write to him now another document and give it to him,

phrases.aramaic: דְּאִי יָהֲבַתְּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא לֵית בַּהּ פְּסֵידָא,
phrases.english: for if you gave to another person there is no loss in it,
for the earlier one acquired.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא אָמְרִינַן לֵיהּ אֲנַן: הַאי כְּתָבָא לָא יָהֲבִינַן לֵיהּ לְהַאי. דִּלְמָא מִכְתָּב כְּתַבְתְּ, מֵיהָב לָא יְהַבְתְּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ, וִיהַבְתַּהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְקָא הָדְרַתְּ בֵּיהּ. אִי לָא יְהַבְתַּהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְקָא בָּעֵית דְּתִתְּבַהּ לְהַאי, כְּתֹב לֵיהּ הַשְׁתָּא כְּתָבָא אַחֲרִינָא וְיַהֲבֵיהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ, דְּאִי יָהַבְתַּהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא לֵית בַּהּ פְּסֵידָא, דְּקָדֵים זָכֵי.

rashi.hebrew: דאי יהבתיה לאיניש אחרינא לית ליה פסידא - בשטרא דכתבת השתא דמאן דקדים שטריה זכה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that if the donor already gave the document to another person, that person suffers no loss because the principle of קדמה זכייה applies to the new document written now.

rashi.hebrew: ביה - שעדיין הוא קיים ואמר תנו לזה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the phrase וקא הדרת ביה means the donor is still alive and is now instructing the court to give the document to this person.

steps.title: Challenge: Both sources discuss שכיב מרע
steps.stepSummary: Rav Zevid challenges the distinction, arguing that both the Mishnah and Baraita refer to שכיב מרע, and proposes a new distinction: the donor himself vs. his son.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב זְבִיד challenges the previous distinction, arguing both sources discuss שכיב מרע. He proposes a new distinction based on who authorizes the return.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב זְבִיד claims both the מִשְׁנָה and בְּרֵיתָא deal with יָיתֵקָאוֹת, which are inherently שכיב מרע cases. He suggests the מִשְׁנָה refers to the donor himself, while the בְּרֵיתָא refers to the donor's son.

keyTerms.meaning: This one refers to him, and that one to his son

steps.whatToRemember: רַב זְבִיד rejects the בריא/שכיב מרע distinction and proposes a new one: donor vs. son.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces the גמרא to re-evaluate the legal principles involved.

phrases.english: Rav Zevid objects to it:

phrases.aramaic: וְהָא אִידֵּי וְאִידֵּי דְּיָיתֵקָאוֹת קָא תָנֵי!
phrases.english: And don't this and that both teach wills!
Rather Rav Zevid said:
This and that are both regarding a deathbed gift,

phrases.aramaic: וְלָא קַשְׁיָא: הָא בֵּיהּ, וְהָא בִּבְרֵיהּ!
phrases.english: And it is not difficult: this is regarding him, and that is regarding his son!

steps.triggerLanguage: מַתְקֵף לַהּ רַב זְבִיד: וְהָא אִידֵּי וְאִידֵּי דְּיָיתֵקָאוֹת קָא תָנֵי! אֶלָּא אָמַר רַב זְבִיד: הָא וְהָא בִּשְׁכִיב מְרַע, וְלָא קַשְׁיָא: הָא בֵּיהּ, וְהָא בִּבְרֵיהּ!

rashi.hebrew: הא בבריה - שמת מחליו וזה בנו אומר החזירהו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the distinction between the Mishnah and the Baraita is that the Mishnah involves the donor himself (הא ביה) while the Baraita involves the donor's son (הא בבריה) authorizing the return after the donor's death.

tosafot.hebrew: והא אידי ואידי דייתיקאות קתני - וא"ת ולפרוך נמי והא מתנות קתני ואור"ת דמיירי מתני' שפירש שנותן לו גוף ופירי מהיום אם לא יחזור עד לאחר מיתה דמצי הדר ביה ורש"י דחק לפרש דגבי מתנות אפי' אם אמר תנו אין נותנין וה"פ דמתניתין שאני אומר כתובין היו ונמלך עליהם שלא ליתנן ויהיב לאיניש אחרינא ועתה רוצה לחזור בו מן השני וליתנה לראשון לפיכך. אפילו אם אמר תנו אין נותנין אבל דייתיקי אם אמר תנו נותנין דאפילו נמלך ונתן לאחר יכול לחזור וקשה לפירושו דא"כ פירוש דנמלך דמתני' יהיה בשני עניינים ותו לעיל גבי גט דדייק הא אמר תנו נותנין אפילו לזמן מרובה לפרש"י ז"ל מנ"ל לפרושי הכי דלמא ה"פ משום דנמלך איכא למיחש שמא לא ניתן הגט עדיין ולכך אפילו אם אמר תנו אין נותנין אלא לשון ונמלך משמע שעתה אינו רוצה כדפרישית לעיל (בבא מציעא י"ח. ד"ה הא):
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions why the Gemara doesn't object that both sources discuss מתנות, and explains that רש״י's interpretation of the Mishnah as a case where the donor changed his mind (נמלך) is difficult because it implies two different meanings for נמלך and conflicts with the earlier discussion of גט.

steps.title: Explanation: Mishnah refers to donor himself
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains Rav Zevid's view: the Mishnah refers to the donor himself, who can retract, so returning the document is safe.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא elaborates on רַב זְבִיד's distinction for the מִשְׁנָה. It explains why the donor himself can authorize the return.
steps.deeperAnalysis: According to רַב זְבִיד, the מִשְׁנָה refers to the donor himself authorizing the return. Since he is a שכיב מרע, he can retract. If he had already given to Person B, returning the first document to Person A causes no loss because the later document prevails.
steps.whatToRemember: The donor himself can authorize the return because he can retract.
steps.whyThisMatters: This explains the מִשְׁנָה under רַב זְבִיד's framework.

phrases.english: He specifically said 'give' — they give,
On his own, it is a matter of retraction, as we say:

phrases.aramaic: אִי נָמֵי יַהֲבַהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא לֵית בַּהּ פְּסֵידָא,
phrases.english: Even if he gave it to another person, there is no loss in it,
First and later — the later one acquires,
For he retracted from the first.

steps.triggerLanguage: דְּקָא אָמַר ״תְּנוּ״ נוֹתְנִין, בְּדִידֵיהּ דְּבַר מִהְדָּר הוּא, דְּאָמְרִינַן: אִי נָמֵי יַהֲבַהּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא לֵית בַּהּ פְּסֵידָא, דְּקַמָּא וּבָתְרָא – בָּתְרָא זָכֵי, דְּהָא הֲדַר בֵּיהּ מִקַּמָּא.

rashi.hebrew: דבר מהדר הוא - ממתנה זו אם חזר בו לאחר שיצאו שני שטרות בב"ד יזכה האחרון:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the term דבר מהדר refers to the donor's ability to retract from the gift after two documents have been issued in court, in which case the later recipient prevails.

rashi.hebrew: א"נ יהבה - שוב לאיניש אחרינא במתנת בריא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the hypothetical scenario of giving to another person refers to a gift made while the donor was healthy, known as מתנת בריא.

rashi.hebrew: לית ליה פסידא - לההוא בתרא בהאי שטרא דמהדרינא ליה להאי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that there is no loss to the second recipient when the first document is returned because the second recipient's claim remains valid.

steps.title: Explanation: Baraita refers to the son
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains Rav Zevid's view: the Baraita refers to the son, who cannot retract his father's gift, so returning the document is unsafe.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא elaborates on רַב זְבִיד's distinction for the בְּרֵיתָא. It explains why the son cannot authorize the return.
steps.deeperAnalysis: According to רַב זְבִיד, the בְּרֵיתָא refers to the son authorizing the return after the father's death. The son cannot retract his father's gift. If the father had already given to Person B, returning the first document to Person A could cause fraud.
steps.whatToRemember: The son cannot authorize the return because he cannot retract his father's gift.
steps.whyThisMatters: This explains the בְּרֵיתָא under רַב זְבִיד's framework.

phrases.english: For it teaches in a baraita:
Even though both of them admit,

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה, בִּבְרֵיהּ.
phrases.english: He shall not return to this one nor to that one, in his own case.
phrases.notes: Refers to a case where the original author of the document has died.

steps.triggerLanguage: כִּי קָא תָנֵי בְּבָרַיְיתָא: אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁשְּׁנֵיהֶם מוֹדִים, לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה, בִּבְרֵיהּ.

steps.title: Scenario: Fraud by the son
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara outlines the scenario where the son might try to fraudulently retract his father's gift by producing an earlier, undelivered document.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains the underlying concern: why might the son want the document returned? It posits a specific scenario of fraud.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא posits a scenario: the father wrote a document for Person A, changed his mind, didn't give it, then the son wrote and gave a document to Person B. Now, the first document is found. The son wants it returned to Person A to trick the court.
steps.whatToRemember: The son's motive is to use the found document to fraudulently retract his father's gift.
steps.whyThisMatters: This scenario explains the potential for fraud and sets up the legal principle that the earlier document prevails in this context.

phrases.english: We say: Perhaps his father wrote it for this one,
and reconsidered and did not give it to him,

phrases.aramaic: וּבָתַר אֲבוּהּ כְּתַב אִיהוּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְיַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ,
phrases.english: and afterward he himself wrote it to another person and gave it to him,
and now he is retracting from that one.

phrases.aramaic: סָבַר: מִהְדָּר לָא מָצֵינָא הָדַרְנָא בִּי.
phrases.english: He thinks: I cannot retract, I will retract from him.

phrases.aramaic: אֵימַר לְהוּ דְּאַבָּא יַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ לְהַאי,
phrases.english: He says to them that his father gave it to this one,
and they will return his deeds to him,

phrases.aramaic: וְנֵיזִיל וְנַפֵּיק מִינֵּיהּ דְּהוּא זָכֵי וְנִפְלוֹג בַּהֲדֵיהּ.
phrases.english: and we will go and take it from him, for he is successful, and we will divide…

steps.triggerLanguage: דְּאָמְרִינַן: דִּלְמָא כְּתַב אֲבוּהּ לְהַאי, וְאִמְּלִיךְ וְלָא יַהֲבַיהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ, וּבָתַר אֲבוּהּ כְּתַב אִיהוּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְיַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ, וְהַשְׁתָּא קָא הָדַר בֵּיהּ מֵהָהוּא. סָבַר: מִהְדָּר לָא מָצֵינָא הָדַרְנָא בִּי. אֵימַר לְהוּ דְּאַבָּא יַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ לְהַאי, וְנִתְּבוּ לֵיהּ כְּתָבֵיהּ, וְנֵיזִיל וְנַפֵּיק מִינֵּיהּ דְּהוּא זָכֵי וְנִפְלוֹג בַּהֲדֵיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: כתבה איהו - הבן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the phrase 'כתבה איהו' refers to the son writing the document, not the father.

rashi.hebrew: דהוא קדים - שיהא שטרו קודם דכיון דמת אביו מחליו זכה משנתנה לו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the son's plan relies on the principle that the earlier document (the father's) takes precedence, and since the father died, the first recipient acquired the property upon delivery.

rashi.hebrew: ונפלוג בהדיה - אחלוק עמו בקנוניא זו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י interprets 'ונפלוג בהדיה' as the son intending to collude with the first recipient to split the property through this fraudulent scheme.

tosafot.hebrew: ובתר אבוה כתבה איהו לאיניש אחרינא - תימה אמאי לא אמרינן נמי שנתנה האב תחלה במתנת שכיב מרע ואח"כ האב עצמו כתבה להאי ולא יהבה ליה ומת האב ונתקיימה המתנה לראשון והבן רוצה שתהיה לשני:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions why the Gemara doesn't consider a scenario where the father first gave a gift on his deathbed (מתנת שכיב מרע) to the first person, then later wrote a document to the second person before dying, which would also validate the first gift and allow the son to favor the second recipient.

steps.title: Court's response: Preventing fraud
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains the court's response: they will not return the document, but will instruct the son to write a new one if he is truthful.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains why the בְּרֵיתָא prohibits returning the document in the son's case. It details the court's preventive measure.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The court will not return the found document to Person A. Why? Because the son might be lying. If the son is truthful and wants to give to Person A, he should write a new document now. If he had already given to Person B, Person B incurs no loss because the earlier document will prevail.

keyTerms.meaning: We will not give it to him

steps.whatToRemember: The court prevents fraud by refusing to return the old document and requiring a new one.
steps.whyThisMatters: This explains the בְּרֵיתָא's ruling not to return the document in the case of the son.

phrases.english: Therefore we say to him:

phrases.aramaic: הַאי כְּתָבָא לָא יָהֲבִינַן לֵיהּ לְהַאי,
phrases.english: This deed we do not give to this one,

phrases.aramaic: דְּדִלְמָא מִכְתָּב כַּתְבֵיהּ אֲבוּהּ מֵיהָב לָא יַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ,
phrases.english: lest perhaps your father wrote it but did not give it to him,

phrases.aramaic: וִיהַבְתֵּיהּ אַתְּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְקָא הָדְרַתְּ בֵּיהּ.
phrases.english: and you gave it to another person and now you are retracting it.

steps.triggerLanguage: הִלְכָּךְ אָמְרִינַן לֵיהּ אֲנַן: הַאי כְּתָבָא לָא יָהֲבִינַן לֵיהּ לְהַאי, דְּדִלְמָא מִכְתָּב כַּתְבֵיהּ אֲבוּהּ מֵיהָב לָא יַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ, וִיהַבְתֵּיהּ אַתְּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא וְקָא הָדְרַתְּ בֵּיהּ.

steps.title: Instruction: Write a new document
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara concludes that if the son is truthful, he should write a new document, as the earlier document will prevail, causing no loss to the second recipient.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא concludes the explanation of רַב זְבִיד's view. It details the instruction to write a new document.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If the son is truthful and wants to give to Person A, he should write a new document now. If he had already given to Person B, Person B incurs no loss because the earlier document will prevail. This ensures that the son's attempt to retract is prevented, and the second recipient is protected.

keyTerms.meaning: If you are telling the truth

keyTerms.term: זִיל אַתְּ הַשְׁתָּא כְּתֹב לֵיהּ שְׁטָרָא אַחֲרִינָא
keyTerms.meaning: Go now and write him another document

steps.whatToRemember: The son must write a new document to prevent fraud.
steps.whyThisMatters: This completes the explanation of רַב זְבִיד's distinction.

phrases.english: But if you are telling the truth,
that your father gave it to him –

phrases.aramaic: זִיל אַתְּ הַשְׁתָּא כְּתֹב לֵיהּ שְׁטָרָא אַחֲרִינָא,
phrases.english: go now and write for him another deed,

phrases.aramaic: דְּאִי נָמֵי לָא יַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ אֲבוּהּ
phrases.english: for even if your father did not give it to him
and you wrote it for another person –
there is no loss in it,
for first and later – the first one acquires.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא אִי קוּשְׁטָא קָא אָמְרַתְּ, דִּיהַב לֵיהּ אֲבוּךְ – זִיל אַתְּ הַשְׁתָּא כְּתֹב לֵיהּ שְׁטָרָא אַחֲרִינָא, דְּאִי נָמֵי לָא יַהֲבַהּ לֵיהּ אֲבוּהּ וּכְתַבְתֵּיהּ אַתְּ לְאִינִישׁ אַחֲרִינָא – לֵית בַּהּ פְּסֵידָא, דְּקַמָּא וּבָתְרָא – קַמָּא זָכֵי.

rashi.hebrew: קמא זכה - מאחר ששני השטרות יצאו משמך הקודם זכה שמתנת בריא הם והשתא דאוקימנא טעמא דלא יחזיר משום דלמא כתבה להאי ואמליך ולא יהבה ליה והדר כתבה לאיניש אחרינא ליכא למרמי אמתנות ולא תידוק מתני' גבי מתנות הא אמר תנו נותנין דכי נמי אמר תנו שייך למימר טעמא דלא יחזיר משום שאני אומר כתובין היו ונמלך עליהן שלא לתנן והדר כתבה לאיניש אחרינא וגבי דייתקאות ע"כ אם אמר תנו נותנין אם הוא קיים דהא תלה תנא דידן טעמא משום שאני אומר נמלך עליהן שלא לתנן ואי נמי הכי הוה דנמלך השתא אם אמר תנו נותנין דליכא למיחש למידי וה"ק מצא דייתיקי או מתנה לא יחזיר שאני אומר כו' והיכא דאמר תנו שייך למימר לא יחזיר משום טעמא דשאני אומר כגון מתנת בריא או בשכיב מרע ומת ובריה קאמר תנו שמעי' ממתני' דלא יחזיר ולא דייקינן האמר תנו נותנין והיכא דלא שייך למימר שאני אומר כגון בשכיב מרע שלא מת מחליו דייקינן האמר תנו דבטל שאני אומר הלכך נותנין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the principle קמא זכה applies here because both documents are considered מתנות בריא, and he clarifies that the reason the son cannot retract is to prevent the concern that he might have written to one person, changed his mind, and then written to another, a logic that applies to both מתנות בריא and שכיב מרע cases where the father is still alive, but differs when the father has died or the son says תנו.

steps.title: Baraita: Finding a receipt
steps.stepSummary: The baraita establishes the rule that if a wife admits to a debt being paid, the finder returns the receipt to the husband, but if she does not admit, it is not returned to either party.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a בְּרֵיתָא stating the הלכה regarding a found שובר. It distinguishes between two cases based on the wife's admission.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרֵיתָא distinguishes between two cases: when the wife admits the debt was paid, the שובר is returned to the husband; when she does not admit, it is not returned to either party. This sets the stage for the גמרא's analysis of the underlying logic.
steps.whatToRemember: The בְּרֵיתָא's rule hinges on the wife's admission.
steps.confusionAlert: The term שׁוּבָר refers to a receipt for payment, often of a כְּתוּבָה.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the baseline הלכה that the גמרא will analyze and challenge.

phrases.english: The Sages taught: One who found a receipt,
at the time that the woman admits — he returns to the husband.
The woman does not admit —

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה.
phrases.english: he does not return neither to this one nor to that one.

steps.triggerLanguage: תנו רבנן: מצא שובר, בזמן שהאישה מודה – יחזיר לבעל. אין האישה מודה – לא יחזיר לא לזה ולא לזה.

rashi.hebrew: מצא שובר - שכתבה אשה לבעלה התקבלתי כתובתי ועודה תחתיו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the שובר in question is a receipt written by the wife to her husband acknowledging receipt of her כתובה, which is still in his possession.

tosafot.hebrew: מצא שובר כו' וליחוש דלמא כתב ליתן בניסן כו' - וא"ת לפרוך על מתני' דגט פשוט (ב"ב דף קסז.) דכותבין שובר לאשה וכ"ת לא חיישינן אלא היכא שנפל ואתרע הא פריך לעיל (בבא מציעא יב:) גבי כותבין שטר ללוה אע"ג דליכא ריעותא וי"ל דמצי לדחויי בשטרי הקנאה אבל הכא אי בשטרי הקנאה מיירי כי אין האשה מודה נמי יחזיר לבעל דאין אשה מודה משמע אפילו אינה אומרת שהוא מזויף אלא אומרת שכתבה ליתן ולא נתנה ואומרת עתה אל תתנו לפי שנמלכה דומיא דמצא שוברין דמתניתין דלא יחזיר שאני אומר כתובים היו ונמלך כו':
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות raises a difficulty regarding why we don't worry that the receipt was written for a future payment (e.g., in ניסן) and answers by distinguishing between cases of inherent suspicion (ריעותא) and those without, ultimately suggesting that if the case involved שטרי הקנאה, the husband would receive the receipt even if the wife doesn't admit, but since the baraita says not to return it, it must be a case where the wife claims she changed her mind about giving the money, similar to the Mishnah's case of found receipts.

steps.title: Challenge: Wife might have sold the ketubah
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the baraita's rule by suggesting the wife might have sold the ketubah rights before giving the receipt, creating a conflict with the buyer.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against the בְּרֵיתָא's instruction to return the שובר to the husband. It posits a scenario where the wife sold the כְּתוּבָה rights.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא posits a scenario where the wife wrote the שובר in Nisan but didn't give it until Tishrei, and in the interim sold the כְּתוּבָה rights. If the שובר is returned to the husband, he could use it to repossess property from the buyer, causing a conflict. This challenges the בְּרֵיתָא's assumption that returning the שובר is straightforward.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge hinges on the possibility of the wife selling the כְּתוּבָה rights before handing over the שובר.
steps.confusionAlert: The term טֶרֶף refers to repossessing property to satisfy a debt.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces the גמרא to consider the legal status of a debt forgiveness when the document is in a third party's hands.

phrases.aramaic: בִּזְמַן שֶׁהָאִשָּׁה מוֹדֶה מִיהַת יַחְזִיר לַבַּעַל,
phrases.english: At the time that the woman admits, at least he will return to the husband,
And let us suspect that perhaps she wrote to give in Nisan,
And she did not give until Tishrei,

phrases.aramaic: וַאֲזַלָה זְבֵנְתַּהּ לִכְתוּבְּ[תַ]הּ בְּטוֹבַת הֲנָאָה מִנִּיסָן עַד תִּשְׁרֵי.
phrases.english: And she went and sold it for her ketubah for financial benefit from Nisan until Tishrei.
And he brings out for him a receipt that is written in Nisan,
And comes to repossess the purchases unlawfully!

steps.triggerLanguage: בזמן שהאישה מודה מיהת יחזיר לבעל, וליחוש דילמא כתבה ליתן בניסן, ולא נתנה עד תשרי, ואזלה זבנתה לכתובתה בטובת הנאה מניסן עד תשרי. ומפיק ליה לשובר דכתב בניסן, ואתא למיטרף לקוחות שלא כדין!

rashi.hebrew: ולא נתנה עד תשרי - והוא לא פרע עד תשרי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the husband did not pay the ketubah until תשרי, explaining the delay in handing over the receipt.

rashi.hebrew: ואזלה - איהי בעודה תחתיו בין ניסן לתשרי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the wife sold the ketubah rights while still married to the husband, between ניסן and תשרי.

rashi.hebrew: וזבנתה לכתובה - לאחר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the wife sold the ketubah to a third party.

rashi.hebrew: בטובת הנאה - כלומר בזול לפי שנותן מעותיו בספק שאם תמות היא יירשנה בעלה ויפסיד מי שלקחה ואם ימות בעלה או יגרשנה יהיה לוקח במקומה ויגבה כתובתה ושוב לא היה לבעלה לפרוע לה הכתובה אלא ללוקח ושובר שנכתב בשמה אינו כלום:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the sale was at a discount because the buyer assumed risk: if the wife died, the husband would inherit the ketubah value and the buyer would lose out, but if the husband died or divorced her, the buyer would collect the ketubah and the husband would no longer owe it, rendering the receipt in the wife's name worthless.

rashi.hebrew: ומפיק לשובר - שכתוב בניסן ויקדום לשטרו של לוקח ויחזיק הבעל בקרקע המיוחדת לכתובתה שלא כדין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the receipt dated ניסן would take precedence over the buyer's document, allowing the husband to unlawfully retain the land designated for the ketubah.

steps.title: רַבָּא's resolution: שְׁמוּאֵל's law of debt forgiveness
steps.stepSummary: Rava resolves the challenge by citing Shmuel's law that a creditor can forgive a debt even if the document is sold, meaning the wife's forgiveness is valid.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּא resolves the challenge by invoking שְׁמוּאֵל's halacha. This is a direct תירוץ to the previous difficulty.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּא argues that the ברייתא's lack of concern for the buyer implies שְׁמוּאֵל's law is accepted. A creditor can forgive a debt even if the document was sold, so the wife's forgiveness is valid. The husband can use the receipt without unlawfully repossessing from the buyer.
steps.whatToRemember: רַבָּא uses the ברייתא as a proof for שְׁמוּאֵל's law.
steps.confusionAlert: שְׁמוּאֵל's law is about the personal nature of the debt obligation.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes a key halachic principle about debt forgiveness and document transfer.

phrases.english: Rava said: Learn from it:
There is the matter of Shmuel, who said Shmuel:
One who sells a debt document to his fellow,
and returns and forgives it — it is forgiven,
and even an heir forgives.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר רבא: שמע מינה: איתא לדשמואל, דאמר שמואל: המוכר שטר חוב לחבירו, וחזר ומחלו – מחול, ואפילו יורש מחול.
